data.title: Meritage Homes Remains A Top Tier Play
data.description:
In my view, the housing market is one of the most interesting spaces to play in when it comes to the stock market right now. This is because there are two opposing forces, and one of them must eventually win. On the one hand, you have years of inflationary pressures and high interest rates aimed at combatting them that have resulted in declining home sales and, over the last couple of years now, a drop in home prices. But on the other hand, you have a perpetual large shortage of homes, with the number of units short amounting to 4.7 million. 8.1 million families are living with somebody who is not related to them. My own view is that the oncoming recession will cause a rapid decline in interest rates. We might see some deflation associated with the materials that go into home building. And these two activities will go a long way toward making houses more affordable, leading to a boom in production.

One of the best players in this market to take into consideration as we lead up to that point is Meritage Homes (MTH). In my last article about the company, published in early October of 2025, I acknowledged industry weakness. But at the end of the day, shares were cheap enough and my belief in how this industry will change moving forward with strong enough to justify maintaining my ‘strong buy’ rating on the stock. Since then, shares have pulled back by 1.6%. That is admittedly worse than the 2.1% rise that the S&P 500 saw. But honestly, I still firmly believe in my previous assessment.

Naturally, I will change my mind as new data comes in. And that new data is just around the corner. After the market closes on January 28th, the management team at Meritage Homes will be announcing financial results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Unfortunately, we should expect more of the same. But so long as something major and unexpected is not to happen that negatively affects the business, I am unlikely to change my assessment when those results come out.

A solid play in this environment

The only new data that investors currently have when it comes to Meritage Homes covers through the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue was $1.42 billion. Not surprisingly, this was below the $1.60 billion the company reported a year earlier. This decline was driven by a drop in the number of homes closed. This number contracted from 3,942 units in 2024 to 3,685 units in 2025. But that's not all. The average price of a home closed also dropped, contracting from $402,000 to $380,000. This perfectly reflects my introduction to this article where I talked about broader market conditions. And it is part of a trend that has been happening for a couple of years now.

Unfortunately, declining revenue is not the only problem that companies have in this market. They often have relatively high fixed costs that they experience deleveraging of sales from, and many home builders are having to contend with the unenviable position of offering higher incentives in order to get buyers to bite. This helps to explain why, even though the decline in revenue was only 10.8%, net income dropped a whopping 49.3%, falling from $196 million down to $99.3 million. Other profitability metrics contracted also. Although the worsening for operating cash flow was modest, with it going from negative $92 million to negative $96.5 million, the drop when it comes to adjusted operating cash flow was sharp. It plummeted from $221.9 million to $131.1 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company dropped from $245.8 million to $126.1 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of 2024. Once again, revenue and profits declined. Cash flows largely worsened also. This is part of a trend for the company driven by weak industry conditions. And even though some of the data has held up better than what I have seen from other players in the space, the company still reported declining homes closed and closing prices.

These aren't the only data points that are problematic. Backlog, for instance, has taken quite a hit. At the end of the third quarter of 2025, Meritage Homes had backlog of 1,699 homes. That was down from the 2,284 homes in backlog reported for the third quarter of 2024. What's more, the average price of a home in backlog dropped from $408,000 to $394,000.

The good news is that there were a couple of bright spots. Even though the cancellation rate for the institution worsened from 10% in the third quarter of 2024 to 11% in the third quarter of 2025, that is far better than it could have been. I have seen some home builders, over the last few years, periodically have cancellation rates in excess of 20%. So to see only a small increase during such a difficult time for the industry is a testament to the quality of the business.

We also can't forget net new orders. And this is where a real bright spot for the company is. The firm went from having 3,512 new homes ordered in the third quarter of 2024 to having 3,636 homes ordered the same time of 2025. A similar year over year increase occurred when it came to the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time a year earlier. But even these data points have a bit of bad to them. I say this because, as the chart below illustrates, the average price of a home ordered dropped, falling from $406,000 in the third quarter of 2024 to $389,000 in 2025. A similar drop can be seen when looking at the nine months of each fiscal year provided.

If we project out results based on what we have seen already for 2025, then we would get net profits for the year of $483.3 million. Adjusted operating cash flow would be about $549.3 million, while EBITDA would come in at $602.8 million. Using these estimates, and also using the results that the company achieved for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart below how the company is currently priced.

In the chart below, meanwhile, you can see how the firm is priced compared to five other similar firms. The pricing is not fantastic on a relative basis, even if it is very appealing on an absolute basis. On a price to earnings basis, for instance, four of the five companies ended up being cheaper than our target is. And three ended up being cheaper when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach. Only on a price to earnings basis was Meritage Homes incredibly compelling, coming in cheaper than any of the five banks that I stacked it up against.

When relative valuation looks like this, a very bullish outlook is typically not the route that I go. But there is another reason I am bullish about the company. This is the fact that the $1.10 billion in net debt on its books means that its overall leverage is very low. In the chart below, you can see how the net leverage ratio of the business of 1.82 stacks up against other comparable firms. Only one of these is better off than it is on this basis. The others have more leverage, with some of them having substantially more.

In addition to having this strong balance sheet, the company has another thing going forward. And this is a fact that a large and growing share of the lots that it has are already owned. Typically, when times get tough, you would prefer that a larger share of lots are optioned. But when you have low leverage and a large portfolio of lots, it means that the company is in a good position from a long term standpoint. It means it can weather the storm that it's in, and that when property prices start to rise again, it will be well positioned. It also means the company has significant financial flexibility with which to grow while property prices are on the cheap. As the image below illustrates, it controls 80,836 lots. That's up from the 74,819 reported the previous year. This works out to about 5.3 years of supply, and 69% of those lots are owned compared to the 64% that were owned a year prior.

At the start of this article, I mentioned that management will be announcing financial results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year after the market closes on January 28th. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue will come in at around $1.51 billion. This would represent a drop from the $1.62 billion the company reported a year earlier. And with that decline in sales would also come a drop in profits, with earnings per share expected to plummet from $4.72 to $1.57. This would mean a decline in net income from $172.6 million to only $111.8 million. In the table below, you can see certain cash flow metrics for the final quarter of 2024. In all likelihood, these metrics will worsen also.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I would say that while market conditions might weaken, and fundamental performance might lag, Meritage Homes is well positioned. The company is cheap on an absolute basis and its net debt situation is positive. While it is possible that upcoming earnings figures might change my mind, it would take a lot for that to happen. At the end of the day, I believe that maintaining a ‘strong buy’ rating is the appropriate move.

data.title: Carlisle Companies: Residential Construction Driven Momentum
data.description:
Introduction and Investment Thesis

I am bullish on Carlisle Companies Incorporated (NYSE: CSL), driven by a projected low federal interest rate ranging between 3.5% to 3.75% from 3.8% at the end of 2025. This is set to benefit home builders by reducing the cost of construction loans used to finance new residential houses. Recent research done on 1,000 participants indicates that over 88% of participants are looking forward to purchasing a home in 2026. Only 78% are a bit indifferent due to affordability, but 58% state that lowered mortgage rates would expedite their plans to own a residential house.

In my opinion, when builders experience market tailwinds such as reduced interest rates and lowered mortgage rates, it becomes affordable to build more residential houses. Now that the mortgage rate is expected to drop from 6.7% in 2025 to 6.0% in 2026, I believe this is likely to improve the demand for residential houses, which is a gain for manufacturers of construction materials. Let me give you a different picture. About 44% of millennials in the U.S. are willing to own a home by committing half of their income to housing. This includes about 49% willing to consider even when the interest rate is above 6%. This indicates that if the interest rate, which is around 3.75% drops to the projected 3.5% as previously stated, there will be an increase in residential house construction at affordable rates. Adding mortgage reduction, I believe that 44% of 74 million millennials, who are 32 million of them, have a chance to own homes in 2026.

Looking back after this promising market tailwind, Carlisle’s revenues are majorly generated from construction materials at 74% and only 26% are generated from weatherproofing technologies. This is a clear indication that the company is best positioned for this residential housing demand. From what I see, I believe this is the optimism that has resulted in Carlisle’s YTD price return at 12.11% beating the S&P 500 at 0.44% since January 2026.

Let me now dive deeper into why I think residential constructions are a major driver of Carlisle’s revenue and why I believe it warrants a bullish rating.

Company Brief

Carlisle Companies Incorporated manufactures building envelope products globally. This company operates through Carlisle Construction Materials and Carlisle Weatherproofing Technologies. It offers roofing systems, insulation, waterproofing, and air vapour barriers, HVAC solutions, and coatings under brands such as panels, Hunter Panels, and so forth. The company was incorporated in 1917, and it is headquartered in Scottsdale, Arizona.

Residential Construction Rebound In 2026

Before I get to the depth of residential construction rebound, I will begin by giving a broader picture. From a broader perspective, the U.S. residential construction market is growing steadily at a CAGR of 4.53% between 2026 and 2031. The current U.S. residential construction market volume is valued at $1.41 trillion, and it is estimated to reach $1.76 trillion by 2031.

By type of construction, 69.05% of overall U.S residential construction is new-build activity. It demonstrates that the demand for new residential houses is there. This is mainly driven by the falling mortgage rates and about 13 million millennial households who are actively in need of residential houses. Right now, the rising housing costs are a major financing challenge delaying the housing sector, but the supply challenge is also to a larger extent, an issue. The U.S. government is beginning with addressing the supply challenge by lowering interest rates and mortgage rates. This supply issue has been discussed so far by the Trump Administration which has resulted to a recent directive of $200 billion mortgage-backed securities purchase with intent of lowering 2026 mortgage rates. Adding that the interest rate is projected to reach 3.5% this year, this might spur the financial aspect of residential housing construction. As a result, the construction financing loans are likely to go down in 2026. This forms the basis of why I believe there are high chances of high demand for construction materials from both new constructions and renovations.

In 2025, the construction investments for both residential and nonresidential constructions were sluggish due to high material costs and uncertainty in policies. Residential constructions have been significantly affected by this lagging and relatively volatile spending, but I believe that this is likely to ease in 2026 following the lowered interest rate and mortgage reductions which are likey to drive an upward spiral. For instance, the nationwide existing home sales are estimated to hit 14% in 2026, up from 0% in 2025, and new home sales are expected to show a 5% gain, up from -2%.

From an inward perspective, Carlisle is positioned to benefit from this potential residential construction growth. I earlier stated that so far, the majority of revenues, at 74% are generated from construction materials and the remaining weatherproofing technologies at 26%. Given that 67% of these revenues are from construction materials for remodeling and replacement, and 33% on new construction, I think Carlisle is best positioned for a potential broadening new construction materials demand likely to soar in 2026.

Residential revenue represents around 8% which can be linked to persistent high housing costs that scaled down the demand for housing. With the projected financial solution (Interest rate reduction) and supply solution (Mortgage rate reduction), I believe the company has what it takes to gain from this projected demand in the residential building materials segment.

So far, Carlisle Companies has been awarded by Extel as the most honored company for the third consecutive year. This indicates Carlisle’s strong performance in corporate leadership in the construction business. In 2025, the company was ranked as the first out of eight categories in the Homebuilder & Products sector, which shows its consistent excellence and credibility in the construction materials sector.

Financials and Valuation

Carlisle’s top-line trend has exhibited volatility over the last couple of years, which demonstrates the uncertainties around housing. For example, there is a significant increase in revenues of $4.45 billion, up from -3.36% YoY growth in 2021 to 42.03% growth YoY in 2022. Notably, a significant fluctuation of revenues followed in 2023 with revenue dipping by about 15.8% YoY, before a 9% YoY growth rebound in 2024.

This signals the cyclical and volatility nature of the housing industry driven by several factors such as unstable interest rates over the last couple of years.

Currently, the revenue is trailing at $5.015 billion, and according to consensus estimates, the revenue for FY2025 is likely to plateau at $5.00 billion. Both federal interest rates and mortgage rates have been above 3.75% and 6.7% respectively, and have slowed home building due to the high costs of borrowing. Now, with an initiative such as $200 billion in the purchase of mortgage-based bonds, this is a blueprint of residential housing affordability, especially to around 32 million millennials in America actively looking for a residential house.

On profitability, the same fluctuation in revenue is also seemingly having an equal impact on the bottom line. Since 2022, as I noted above on the top-line, the bottom-line has been fluctuating, indicating the housing sector’s volatility. But the net income trend shows that Carlisle has a solid execution capability, looking at 2022 revenues, which were the highest at $4.45 billion and a net income of $924 million. Compared to FY2024, when the revenue hit $5.003 billion, the net income was a 5-year high at $1.311 billion, indicating strong execution. Notably, for more than 5 years, the company has consistently maintained profitability despite the macroeconomic uncertainties which further supports its strong execution.

I believe that moving forward, the current adjusted federal interest rates and reduced mortgage rates previously stated will be a major driver of this company’s earnings rebound. This is because the net income still lags that of 2024, trailing at $776 million, down from $1.312 billion in 2024. The historical trend in the last 5 years shows that Carlisle’s EPS has been beating analysts’ estimates. With FY2026 backing of strong fundamentals, I believe there is a grounded reason to believe in the possibility of EPS beating 2026 estimates. This projected growth in 2026 is likely to drive an EPS of about $25.

I will now value CSL using the P/S multiple to that of its peers, Armstrong World Industries, Inc. (NYSE: AWI), and Owens Corning (NYSE: OC). CSL P/S ratio is at 3.12x, which is below AWL’s P/S ratio of 5.15x, and a distance higher than OC, which is at 0.91x.

With the market tailwinds and market leadership CSL has demonstrated over the years and possible dominance in the supply of residential construction materials, I think the market undervalues CSL. Well, AWI has a higher revenue growth rate YoY for the FY2026 at 6.91% resulting in $1.75 billion, compared to CSL, whose revenue growth rate YoY for FY2026 is 2.37% resulting in $5.12 billion. Clearly, CSL is demonstrating market dominance, which is why I think this is an undervaluation. OC has a revenue growth rate for the FY2026 of -3.35% representing $9.79 billion, which also indicates that CSL is dominating the construction material market. As such, I think CSL has a high upside potential given its best market positioning, which is why I am optimistic. In my view, a revaluation to about 5x is plausible.

Investment Risk

According to the investor presentation, only 33% of Carlisle Construction Materials’ revenue and 56% of Carlisle Weatherproofing Technologies’ revenue come from new construction, with the majority tied to remodeling and replacement. So far, new constructions are facing delays, affordability challenges, and cancellations due to high interest rates and weakened consumer spending. If the interest rates and mortgage rates do not reduce as projected, these conditions may persist or worsen in 2026. This could affect Carlisle’s $5.0 billion revenue base and its Vision 2030 target of around 5% organic growth despite recording a solid re-roofing demand.

Conclusion

I have a bullish stance on this company, and I hinge on possible interest rate reduction and looming mortgage reduction following $200 billion purchase of mortgage bonds. This is a hint of a potentially strong 2026 residential housing demand, which will spur the demand for construction materials.

data.title: We Could Be In For A Flat Market In 2026
data.description:
Flat 2026? October Was The Nasdaq's last high. What to do?

Where are we headed? The Nasdaq is flat, and the S&P 500 is nearly so.

In this article, I want to explore how important the biggest names on the Nasdaq and the S&P 500 are to the state of US-based equities. Without the 10 “Trillionaires” on an upswing, who will propel the market to new highs? I am not saying a down or flat market is a certainty, as always, I am talking about risks. No one is predicting a bear market for 2026, and I am not making that case here. I am just putting the notion that the indexes could be flat for 2026 on the table and evaluating the risks. You are going to hear me say risk several times in this article, as someone who actively manages my stock portfolio, risk is extremely important. Most of my loyal readers at this point have heard me mention this before. Since it’s a new year, I hope you don’t mind if I talk about how I approach risk again. I have talked about how I use cash as part of an active hedging strategy and also how I use options and inverse ETFs for hedging. Using options is a very powerful tool, and before you toy with them, go to your brokerage firm’s education section and learn as much as you can about them before you use them. Now with that PSA out of the way, let’s summarize: I am not predicting a bear market, I am not even stating that a flat market is a certainty. I am writing about this topic because I am thinking through what might be ahead of us. That, despite the optimism in the financial media, I want to build my own picture of where the market is going. Again, if you have been reading my content for a while, I am always saying “this, or that will happen.” Then, as the week or month transpires, I compare what is happening to what I thought would happen and adjust accordingly.

I have been sharing a much more positive view with my investment group. For instance, I was taking for granted that 2025 would see 7000 to 7200 on the S&P 500. I was expecting a strong year-end rally, but the Santa Claus Rally was barely there, especially for the Nasdaq. The large year-end rally started early in October, reaching 6986, and then fizzled out. I think it is right and proper for a stock optimist like me to examine whether the first half (or even all) of 2026 might be treading water for the larger indexes. The state of being bullish is not the same as belonging to a team or a religion, so thinking about the underperformance of the stock indexes is not disloyalty. The only thing I am loyal to is growing the value of my portfolio. I will probably say this more than twice. I am talking about risks, not certitude.

It is fantastic that the rest of the market is outperforming, but take out these 10, then the market has to pull that 40% to 60% weight along with it. The alternative to up is not only down but staying in place. As a close observer of the stock market, I have to weigh the risks to the overall market. As always, the general trend of the market influences nearly all stock valuation is up. This is not merely due to the “Animal Spirits” of bullishness but because of the dominance of ETFs. On the other hand, a crash of a few giant stocks mechanically reverberates throughout ETF members and depresses all. Likewise, the upthrust of several megacaps will boost all stocks in that sector because of sector ETFs and the larger indexes. In fact, that is what the Mag-7 and their ilk have been doing for quite a few years. Further stating the obvious, is that they deserved the higher valuation because they have been growing revenue and cash flow from existing businesses and creating new businesses. The Magificent-7 are (were?) truly magnificent. Everyone knows that, and most of us at this point own most of them, if not all. So we are all subject to their performance. Going into the height of earnings season, we are hoping that most participants have been trimming their tech titan holdings, so that if the bigs do outperform, the stocks will start moving up again. The more I think about it, the more this notion makes sense. The big-cap names are fully valued; they will only go higher if they really surprise to the upside. Perhaps their performance could nudge them a bit closer to their old highs, but that isn’t enough.

The most popular stocks tend to be the growth stocks.

In the indexes, the top stocks are weighted over 40% for the S&P 500 (cite) and about 60.41 % for the Nasdaq composite -100(cite). Much is being made of how well the Small caps and the 490 stocks of the S&P 500 perform. I ask you to look at this ratio, and ask yourself, is this really the true picture of the stock market? We take for granted that higher weighting goes to the bigger names, but if everyone has piled into these names, pushing up the P/Es, maybe the tech titans need to grow into those valuations for a bit. Meaning maybe they create cash flow and profits for Q4 and exceed expectations, and even raise expectations, but remain at their current level. I am not predicting a bear market, just perhaps a flat one.

Looking at the Trillionaire Club's recent performance, you might be a bit unimpressed

The issue is whether the indexes can make new highs without the Trillionaires: Nvidia (NVDA), Alphabet (GOOGL), Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Broadcom (AVGO), Meta Platforms (META), Tesla (TSLA), Taiwan Semi (TSM), Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B). Quibble with TSM and BRK.B. The table below shows the weighting in the S&P500 via the S&P500 ETF (SPY). This makes sense as we are talking about ETFs, and also how most people invest in this index. Yes, I mistakenly had TSM on the S&P 500 list even though it is a Taiwanese company. Still, TSM is a component of the Semiconductor index and ETF (SMH), and semiconductors have been the big performers. So even though it’s not in the SPY, it is still in the “Trillionaire Club.” TSM, as with NVDA and other titans, is certainly an influence in all kinds of tech and growth ETFs. TSM stays in the picture.

Almost all of the names below are more than 10% lower than their highs.

Most investors invest in ETFs, and the biggest ones are cap-weighted

Everyone seems to be celebrating the strength of 490 S&P500 stocks compared to the “Trillionaire Club”; I am too. However, as I’ve said, a rally without their leadership seems to be capping the ability for new highs. That is really the key to this whole article, really. This means that owners of these most popular ETFs have a higher risk of underperforming this year for the average investor.

I would not necessarily urge someone who puts money away every month to change in mid-stream, or if you have a money manager to override their advice and sell stocks. Let me say that dollar cost averaging is a powerful way to build wealth, and stick with your plan. However, if you are like my investment group, we actively manage our portfolios. That means that stock picking matters, and how you select stocks matters. However, there are some safe tactics that, if I were starting fresh or allocating new funds to an investment. I would look at some Equal-weight versions of many of the popular cap-weighted indexes.

Now I am not casting shade at the Cap-Weighted indexes and the ETFs that are based on them. They work brilliantly; of course, you want to give more weight to the best stocks. The S&P 500 rearranges the weighting every quarter, which means the best get a bigger share than the slower names. My reasoning for why the big names haven’t gone anywhere but down for most of the last 3 months is that perhaps they are richly but fully valued. The message of the market is that these stocks are so huge and highly valued that they need to accrue a higher level of earnings to justify a higher price. If you want to say that there is a fear that the AI revolution has pulled forward valuation, I won’t dissuade you. I am a firm believer that Generative AI will bring tremendous value to corporate America. That said, there will be bumps along the way; maybe that is what is holding back the appreciation in the bigs.

So if I were looking for an adjustment in my ETF holdings, to allow for smaller stocks to shine through, this is what I would do.

There are equal weight ETF that mimic the S&P 500, and others

  • Equal-Weight S&P 500 (RCP)

  • Equal-Weight Nasdaq 100 (QQQE)

  • Equal-Weight Biotech (XBI)

  • Equal-Weight Semi Conductors (XSD)

Also Look at mid-sized and small cap stocks indexes

  • Midcap Russell ETF (IWR)

  • S&P 600 ETF (IJR)

I have tried to locate the ETFs with the largest number of shares outstanding. There are tons of ETFs out there. If you think the Tech Trillionaires will keep their rapid growth for a few more years, this change in tactic is not for you. At least this thought exercise might open us up to the notion that we may have to look elsewhere for alpha if the Big Caps are stuck in neutral.

Here is a more positive alternative

No one should be feeling bearish after reading this article. If you are a stock picker and you are good at selecting your own stocks, you have a good opportunity to outperform the indexes. Personally, my investment group and I operate totally without using ETFs. We targeted select sectors that I believe are emerging growth and should positively sustain our portfolios in the future. We have been about 2 years early, but we have individual small-cap biotechs, medtech, Space, Defense, and Aviation. Yes, now names like Rocket Labs (RKLB) and Planet Labs (PL) might be familiar, but we accumulated them when they were brand new and quite cheap. Even with all that, I do own the big-cap tech names, and I even recently bought Microsoft (MSFT) this week when it was just at 488.99, a tad below my 20% discount rule, at -19.8% (but good enough). I don’t own AAPL, not a fan, and I don’t own TSLA or BRK.B. That leaves AMZN, GOOGL, NVDA, and META.

I am, however, underweight compared to these big names weighing in the indexes. The bigcaps are about 20% of my portfolio, and I would add to them if they fell into my -20% discount filter. The discount filter rule is just one of the tactics in our trading and investing approach.

What do I really think?

This exercise helped me confirm the notion that if these big caps don’t start moving, it will hurt everyone but the best individual stock investors. I hedged this past week using the inverse Nasdaq-100 ETF (SQQQ) and also using call options for the inverse S&P 500 (SPXS). I made a little money on them, but I am happy to be a loser, knowing that I have a little insurance against a sharp move to the downside. I also set aside cash the week before, in case some great stock like MSFT falls into my 20% discount rule. Speaking of MSFT, they report this week, so I will be ready in case it or any other great stock gives me that great discount. Let me leave you with one more positive thought: all of these big-cap names that are underwater don’t have to make new highs or even hit the old ones. If they lift 5% or more, we could easily make new highs, and everyone will be happy as long as the 490 does their part.

Have a great week, trading everyone!

data.title: Topicus: Recurring VMS Revenues And M&A Discipline Remain Bullish
data.description:
Topicus.com Inc. (TOITF) is a part of the Constellation Software Inc. (CSI) ecosystem. TOITF is a vertical market software (VMS) holding company, with independently managed business units serving end markets that include education, healthcare, government, finance, and legal, among others. The company combines two large families: the Total Specific Solutions (TSS) and the Netherlands-based Topicus platform cluster. Last year, Topicus’s collaborations in healthcare with Better improved by using the same data layer, as well as TSS’s acquisitions of Cipal Schaubroeck and a part of Asseco Poland. And overall, I believe TOITF’s recurring revenue base still justifies a long-term bullish outlook at these levels.

Rolling Up VMS Businesses

Topicus is technically a holding company of vertical market software (VMS) firms across Europe. TOITF has independently managed software business units, operating across several countries, such as the Netherlands, France, Germany, the Nordics, and the United Kingdom, that serve verticals like education, healthcare, government, finance, legal, real estate, and retail. However, TOITF itself is a spin-out from Constellation Software Inc. (CSI), which was created in 2021.

Nevertheless, TOITF remains part of CSI’s ecosystem, and CSI retains an economic participation in TOITF (around 30%). Additionally, CSI also exerts controls on TOITF through its voting rights. I previously covered TOITF in April last year, and in the following months, the stock appreciated nicely. Unfortunately, around July 2025, the stock eventually topped out, and it’s now down approximately 43% from its 2025 highs, which is why I believe it’s worth revisiting this name.

You see, TOITF brings together CSI’s European VMS, which operates as Total Specific Solutions (TSS), with the Netherlands-based Topicus. Topicus clusters platform to develop software designed around the end user experience. This way, they engage clients within a shared digital environment that connects organizations, professionals, and their consumers. In essence, they form a decentralized SaaS ecosystem that targets a wide range of institutional clients.

For example, recently Topicus announced a collaboration in healthcare with Better, a provider of a digital health data platform. Topicus will use this platform to build multiple applications that plug into the same open data layer to make it easier to share patient data across hospitals, primary care, and community care organizations.

Acquisition First

Having said that, Topicus’s TSS is probably one of the company’s most important aspects. This portion of their business buys and builds their vertically integrated systems, which now comprises over 180 independent software companies. TOITF breaks this business down into two sub-groups with 1) TSS Public and 2) TSS Blue. TSS Public focuses on the public sector and healthcare in the Netherlands and the southern parts of Central Europe. On the other hand, TSS Blue specializes in private markets, with a geographic emphasis on the Netherlands, Germany, and the Nordics.

Fortunately, for TOITF, 2025 was again a notable year in terms of M&A. TSS finalized several new deals, ending the year with the acquisition of 23 companies that remained independent and kept their local identity. TSS invested a large amount of capital in two important transactions with Asseco Poland and Cipal Schaubroeck. Normally, TOITF generally buys out companies entirely, but in the Asseco Poland deal, TOITF only acquired a minority position of 24.84%. What’s interesting about Asseco is that it consists of a federation of several vertical market software businesses with local autonomy across more than 60 countries. So, in a way, TOITF bought a stake in a smaller version of itself. Plus, this was TSS's largest investment to date, which shows they’re doubling down on this growth through an M&A approach.

Additionally, TOITF finalized another large transaction in 2025, which was the acquisition of Cipal Schaubroeck. This is a Belgian local government software provider that offers suites for HR, payroll, security, hosting, and data management. Its customer base is centered on Flemish public institutions like municipalities, provinces, police zones, and social welfare. This acquisition complements TSS’s local government software, and it was a meaningful platform-scale in gov-tech that required a payment totaling $258 million.

Valuation And Upside Potential

Now, from a valuation perspective, TOITF currently trades at a $7.0 billion market cap. Their latest financial report (as of September 2025) shows their balance sheet holds $324.6 million in cash and short-term investments, against $873.1 million in financial debt (aside from other regular operating liabilities). Also, TOITF's latest weighted average shares outstanding is around 83.1 million. And their book value stood at $820.0 million, which would indicate a somewhat inflated P/B of 8.5. For comparison, their sector’s median P/B is much lower at around 3.9.

Moreover, using those figures, I estimate TOITF’s EV at around $7.6 billon. And, according to Seeking Alpha’s dashboard on TOITF, the company is projected to generate approximately $2.1 billion in revenues in 2026. So, that would suggest TOITF’s EV/S is roughly 3.65, which is actually slightly lower than its peers’ median forward EV/S of 3.7. So, for the most part, I believe TOITF is probably already trading around its fair value at these levels.

However, I do like that 2026 would imply that the company itself is growing at an above-average clip. Concretely, TOITF's forward topline growth is around 20.8%, which is much higher than the rest of its sector peers' growth of just 8.2%. So, based on that alone, I would actually argue that the stock deserves at the very least a premium above the sector median valuation multiples. Personally, I think a forward EV/S of 4.0 would be only 8.1% higher than its sector multiples, but would result in an implied EV of around $8.4 billion. So, using that figure, I estimate a tentative "fair" market cap of $7.85 billion, and my PPS price target of around $94.5.

Risk Analysis

Having said that, TOITF's cash flows seem to have significant fluctuations quarter over quarter. For instance, its latest Q3 figures suggest they generated only $55.0 million in positive cash flows. Note that I got that estimate by simply adding its quarterly cash flows from operations and CAPEX. Yet, using the same approach and TOITF’s TTM figures (as of September 2025), their positive cash flow increases to around $440.4 million (or $110.1 million per quarter on average). And, in that sense, TOITF would trade at a TTM P/CF multiple of only 15.2.

This is probably another good indicator that they’re now again in a compelling buy zone, as their peers’ P/CF is much higher at 19.1. However, volatile cash flows do make the stock itself riskier as well. And remember, my thesis depends on them executing prudently on their M&A strategy. If TOITF overpays for its acquisitions, while also failing to integrate its newly added subsidiaries, then it would be a double whammy for investors.

So, M&A is easily their main risk, because it often requires successful integration to fully realize its synergies. TOITF somewhat mitigates this risk because it mostly lets its subsidiaries continue business as usual post-acquisition. Nonetheless, even though that isn’t a big factor given TOITF’s business model, sizable write-downs due to a large failed M&A transaction could also send the stock much lower as a result.

Finally, using my figures, it's worth highlighting that TOITF's debt-to-cash ratio of 2.7 is somewhat high already. So, if their cash flow turns into cash burn for a few successive quarters, then it could constrain TOITF significantly. Fortunately, I believe they're a cash flow machine at the moment, which should give them ample ammunition to continue with their growth through M&A strategy. After all, TOITF essentially grows mainly through acquiring other firms. Plus, even though they're decentralized across Europe, if there's a sweeping EU regulation against their business model, you could see a quick pullback in the shares as a reaction.

But ultimately, given their positive cash flows, this growth strategy seems viable for now. Just note that they have less organic growth otherwise, which could backfire if their liquidity and cash flows ever dry up. In fact, only about 3% of its growth was organic, out of the 24% reported YoY growth this latest quarter. Thus, the best way to think of TOITF’s value proposition is through recurring maintenance revenue and client retention, which is then reinvested in further M&A to increase its topline.

Conclusion: Still A Compelling “Buy”

Overall, TOITF’s M&A strategy seems to continue to pay off. So far, they’ve accumulated a sizeable amount of recurring revenues through their acquisitions, mainly through maintenance services. I believe this shows they’re not buying into hype or dreams, but rather judiciously adding more shareholder value over time. In fact, I calculate that this type of recurring sales accounted for around 71.6% of its latest quarterly revenues. In my view, maintenance tends to lead to quite sticky revenues over time, because clients always need to keep their software in working condition regardless of macro cycles. So, given their relatively reasonable valuation multiples, I still think TOITF remains well-positioned to continue accumulating shareholder value over time for long-term investors.

data.title: Lear: A Solid Choice In Automotive Parts
data.description:
I recently updated my coverage on Adient (ADNT), where I gave Lear (LEA) as an alternative to the stock, and called Lear a solid/better choice than Adient for a multitude of reasons. Today, I will be updating on why this is such a solid choice, why I own the stock, and why I believe the company could be a market-beating longer-term investment.

This is not my first coverage of Lear. I have in fact covered the company for years - my last article can be found here, and is about 7 months old at this time. Since my last article, the TSR for the company has been more than twice that of SPY, and more importantly, significantly better than Adient.

You'll also note that it's not my first article on Lear where I am positive. I have been building a position in this company for over a year at a compelling price ($85-$95, roughly). The fact that the company now trades at about $119/share doesn't mean that it's unattractive though, it just means that we have some correction of the valuation, which, since about mid-2024 was troughing. Lear is, in my view, a perfect example of how to go about value-oriented investing, because the cycles for the company tend to be somewhat longer, and it tends to lack some of the immediate volatility of other stocks in the same sector.

In this article i'll revisit my targets for Lear, my expectations for the 2026-2028E period, and show you why, if you want to go into automotive supplies, Lear should be one of your first choices.

The yield and the credit rating are just part of the reason for why the company is attractive. It's not a bad idea to invest in one of the more vertically integrated suppliers in the entire automotive supply value chain - even if the valuation now is, by comparison, much higher than it once was.

Let's see what we have here.

Upside from automotive supply and parts

Like Adient, the company's primary operation is, at least in part, related to seating. Lear's revenues are primarily related to this sub-segment, which is what makes the company directly comparable to Adient - it manufactures everything from the basic structure to mechanisms to foams as well as headrests. It also installs things like heating and cooling in seating, but also focuses on additional features such as safety and intelligent functions. Lear also has its own leather and fabric for specialized covering and materials. Operations are international, but NA is a focal point for the company.

Secondary operational foci include electrical systems - specifically for new vehicle segments, such as BEV. Here, the company manufactures wiring harnesses, connectors, electronics, electrification (including battery management and chargers) as well as connected services.

The fact that the company has such a major part of its operations in seating and the inherent complexity of the seating manufacturing and operations, means that competitors wanting to enter this segment are few and far and few between. The work necessary to build up this sort of operation, not to mention create the confidence with suppliers and customers, can be characterized in well over a decade, with billions and billions of dollars. While some automotive manufacturers are moving to their own seating manufacturer, many still take advantage of what a company like Lear does.

The reasons why Lear is so interesting would include the degree of vertical integration that the company has. What I mean by this is that aside from a few specialized components and parts, the company manufactures most things on an in-house basis, which usually means efficiency, margins and lower costs for consumers.

Recently, Lear has also been investing more heavily in things like data integration, AI, and software automation to be able to cut these costs and enhance these upsides even further. A new term in the industry is lights-out manufacturing, meaning that the plants have the ability to run autonomously.

Lear's returns to shareholders, including repurchases of shares, have been very impressive. Lear is one of the few companies where I believe its buyback strategy is very good - because it focuses it on years or times when the stock is objectively cheap from a historical (and other metrics) perspective.

Most articles for Lear as of late have been positive - but many of the recent ones in 2025, have also been written by me. The upside that I see is partially based on what the company sees it can achieve, and partially a reversal to a fair valuation (which has now, to some extent, happened). The company's targets include an expansion of leadership in its seating operations, coupled with increases in EBIT through focusing of the company portfolio.

The perhaps core risk which I am not a big fan of, is some of the company's growth base. Much of both the expected growth, and the recent growth, has or will come from Chinese OEM's.

This means that Lear is meaningfully correlated (more so than last year), to Chinese OEM's, and that if there's a downturn in Chinese automotive, the growth that's being forecasted is unlikely to materialize as expected or as forecasted by the company.

And anyone arguing that this risk is not significant is, in my view, missing some key parts about Chinese BEV brands. The risks which are evident include significant overcapacity, margin problems due to increasing cuts in pricing, the potential for the Chinese government to reduce automotive market subsidies, which is already happening as we speak, as well as what I believe to be a fundamental lack of likelihood of international expansion (backed by politics, which are pushing against potential expansion) to any degree that would justify a higher valuation.

The reason why I'm not valuing Lear much, much higher, is because I am bearish on Chinese automotive. If you're bullish, then you probably would have a more positive view here, where the company's growth targets of double digits may be met. But because this goes against the data coming out of China, I consider this to be risky.

Lear is still attractive, despite these risks. Recent results mostly confirm this. Top-line results, meaning sales, are up for the last period, and despite a small drop in EPS (Adjusted), company cash flow was up by more than 100% YoY. Both segments saw positive momentum, and the cash flow was the third-ever highest quarter OCF, causing an increase in 2025E FCF. Appeal for its segments and products was confirmed with new business awards - $1.1B of it in the smaller E-systems segment alone.

It also continues to receive awards for seating and system innovations, and it achieves finishes and results that not even Adient can compete with - such as the J.D Power 2025 seat quality/satisfaction study, which it won for the third consecutive year in 2025.

On these bases, I consider this company to be an attractive business to invest in even now, as long as we're getting the company at the right or a cheap price.

What's a cheap price

So, a cheap price, going by my last target of $150/share, is still a long way to go from here. Despite significant returns, and a recovery from double-digit share price levels, I believe Lear at 9.66x P/E to be meaningfully undervalued.

The 5-year average is 14-15x P/E, implying an upside to a fair value target of no less than $240/share, justifying my $150 PT. Even using the longer-term 20-year average P/E as a forecast basis, that puts us at over $190/share, which also justifies my PT at a 19% upside.

And that, dear readers, is the lowest possible target estimate I would consider valid here.

Compare this to the upside that a company like Adient has, with a P/E that depends on 20-30x P/E premiumization on a forward basis. Here we don't depend on that sort of premiumization, and we also get a yield. I believe the only companies in this industry more attractive than Lear, are companies with a significantly different risk/reward ratio, by which I mean higher risk. As an alternative, I could name Valeo (VEOEY), which I technically consider to be more attractive, and with a better yield, but at what risk?

Valeo may have a better upside, but its risk is not appealing given its end markets as well as size. Unlike Lear, where technologies and products are mostly technology-agnostic (meaning they are needed regardless how your vehicle is powered or used), Valeo has more dependencies and also significant other differences, not the least of which is a BB rating instead of investment-grade.

That's why I own Valeo, but at a much smaller stake - and I'm fully cognizant of the risks involved.

My point, dear reader, is know your risk, know your exposure, know your expectations.

Don't invest in something because A says so, and you don't know what to expect. Lear, in my view, is likely to return market-beating rates of return over the medium (5-year) period, while paying an attractive yield, and while not offering, in my view, too much volatility. Unlike some peers like Adient, it does not depend on premiumization; it's a proven business with decades of appeal, and the current trends speak in favor of its valuation.

The risks are there, but the valuation is attractive despite this. Even in a scenario of very little growth, which could happen due to unattractive China trends, you're unlikely to see capital losses - just less profit.

Lear has no debt maturities until 2 years from now, it has low cost of cash at below 4% for its bonds, and has over $3B in 2025A liquidity. Also, it's on track for very solid conversion rates, and it continues to pay dividends. It's a healthy, solid, well-managed company.

The risks to this solid company are mostly external, and are as follows.

Risks to My Thesis

The primary risks to my thesis are related to recovery, or the lack of it, in LVP/automotives. Lear's production cycles are tied to cyclical trends, and when LVP production or demand goes down, as we're seeing now (still, yes, in 2026), downturns are entirely possible. The core risk to Lear here is, as I would characterize it, aside from global LVP, China exposure.

The company has gone on record increasing its China exposure step by step, with a target of almost half by 2027. I believe this to be a mistake on Lear's part, given the automotive specifics of the Chinese market, and the risks coming here. Secondary risks to consider are input costs, namely base metals and components, but these are really secondary.

Beyond these twin risks, I don't see much that could impact Lear in the longer term, and as long as we have automotives, I believe that Lear is not in a place where anything could derail it fundamentally.

My PT of $150/share remains - all of the upside, and all of the risk is included previously, because not much of this is new. What I am doing is a re-iteration following significant outperformance to the market.

Thesis

Lear is a fundamentally attractive business with an investment-grade credit rating, good yield, and conservative upside. Given that the company has spent the last few years moving from a valuation of over 15x P/E to a valuation that still, as of early 2026, is still sub-10x P/E normalized, I still view this company as meaningfully undervalued despite a market-beating TSR of over 30% in less than 6 months.

Because of this, I am maintaining my target for Lear at $150/share. I believe this price target takes into consideration China risks as well as other LVP-related risks we're seeing, including what may come from FX. Because the USD is so attractive to invest in now, with a lower pairing than in over 3 years, I'm looking at buying more of the business.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this and a price target of $150/share, where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Buy" rating as of January 2026.

data.title: Tracking Baillie Gifford's 13F Portfolio - Q4 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Baillie Gifford’s 13F portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on their regulatory 13F Form filed on 01/23/2026. Please visit our Tracking Baillie Gifford 13F Portfolio series to get an idea of their investment philosophy and our last update for the fund’s moves during Q3 2025. The 13F securities represent roughly 40% of their overall Assets Under Management. The portfolio is diversified with around 300 13F securities. 45 of those positions are significantly large (more than ~0.5% of the portfolio) and they are the focus of this article.

This quarter, Baillie Gifford’s 13F portfolio value decreased from ~$135B to ~$120B. The top three holdings are at ~18% while the top five are close to ~27% of the 13F assets: NVIDIA, MercadoLibre, Amazon, Spotify, and Sea Limited.

Note: Although the following positions are really small compared to the overall size of the portfolio, it is significant that they have sizable ownership stakes: American Superconductor (AMSC), Appian Corporation (APPN), Aurora Innovation (AUR), Copa Holdings (COPA), Coursera (COUR), Denali Therapeutics (DNLA), Dutch Bros (BROS), Gingko Bioworks (DNA), Lemonade (LMND), MakeMyTrip (MMYT), Mobileye (MBLY), Oddity Tech (ODD), Recursion Pharma (RXRX), Remitly Global (RELY), Sana Biotech (SANA), Sweetgreen (SG), Tencent Music Entertainment (TME), Upwork (UPWK), and Wayfair Inc. (W).

Stake Increases:

MercadoLibre Inc. (MELI): MELI position goes back to 2010 when a 2.25M share stake was built at prices between ~$35 and ~$73. The position size peaked at 6.56M shares in 2014. The stake increase happened at prices between ~$60 and ~$140. The two years through Q2 2023 saw a ~39% increase at prices between ~$635 and ~$1946. There was a ~11% selling during Q4 2023. Q1 2025 also saw a similar reduction at prices between ~$1740 and ~$2260. It is their second largest 13F position at 5.83% of the portfolio. The stock currently trades at ~$2137. There was a ~12% trimming over the last two quarters while this quarter saw a ~5% stake increase.

Note: they have a ~7% ownership stake in the business.

NU Holdings (NU): The 3.58% NU position saw a ~48% increase during Q1 2024 at prices between $8.09 and $12.25. That was followed by a ~10% increase in the next quarter at prices between $10.48 and $12.92. Q3 2024 also saw a ~13% stake increase at prices between $10.71 and $15.14. The next quarter saw a ~7% further increase at prices between $10.24 and $15.89. Q1 2025 also saw a ~18% stake increase at prices between ~$10 and ~$14. The stock is now at ~$18. There was minor trimming in the last two quarters while in this quarter there was a ~2% stake increase.

Netflix Inc. (NFLX): NFLX is a 2.88% of the portfolio stake established in the 2015-2016 timeframe at prices up to ~$13. There was a ~20% trimming over the two years through Q1 2023. That was followed by a ~15% reduction in the next quarter at prices between ~$32 and ~$45. The three quarters through Q1 2024 saw a ~20% further selling. The last five quarters also saw further reduction while in this quarter there was a minor increase. The stock currently trades at ~$86.

Note: The prices quoted are adjusted for the 10-for-1 stock split in November.

Duolingo (DUOL): The 0.67% of the portfolio position saw a ~29% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$257 and ~$468. That was followed by a ~72% further increase this quarter at prices between ~$166 and ~$353. The stock currently trades below the bottom of those ranges at ~$156. They are building this position against falling prices.

Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), Atlassian Corp (TEAM), Axon Enterprise (AXON), CoStar Group (CSGP), Rocket Lab (RKLB), Samsara (IOT), and The Ensign Group (ENSG): These positions were increased during the quarter.

Stake Decreases:

NVIDIA Inc. (NVDA): A large stake in NVDA was first purchased in 2016 at prices up to ~$28. The position has seen periodic selling since. Recent activity follows. Q2 2024 saw a ~20% reduction at prices between ~$76 and ~$136. That was followed by a ~23% reduction during the next quarter at prices between ~$99 and ~$135. Q4 2024 also saw a ~17% selling at prices between ~$117 and ~$149. The next quarter saw another ~11% reduction at prices between ~$107 and ~$149. The stock currently trades at ~$188 and the stake is the largest 13F position at 6.80% of the portfolio. The last two quarters saw minor trimming.

Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN): AMZN stake was a very small position purchased in 2004. The period through 2009 saw a large position built at very low prices. Since 2014, the position has seen selling. Recent activity follows. The last three years saw minor trimming most quarters. The stock is now at ~$239, and the stake is at 5.64% of the portfolio.

Shopify Inc. (SHOP): The 4.17% of the portfolio SHOP stake was built in the 2017-2019 timeframe at very low prices. Since then, the position had seen minor trimming. Recent activity follows. There was a ~10% selling during Q2 2024 while in the next quarter there was a ~15% stake increase at prices between ~$52 and ~$81. Q2 2025 saw a roughly one-third reduction at prices between ~$70 and ~$117. There was a ~6% trimming during the last quarter. This quarter also saw a ~12% selling at prices between ~$136 and ~$182. The stock currently trades at ~$138. They are harvesting gains.

SEA Ltd. (SE): The 4.11% position in SE was increased by 20% during Q3 2023 at prices between ~$35 and ~$67. The next quarter saw another 31% increase at prices between ~$35 and ~$47. Q3 2024 saw a further ~15% stake increase at prices between ~$60 and ~$95. The stock is now at ~$125. The last few quarters have seen minor trimming.

Note: they have a ~7% ownership stake in SEA Ltd.

Spotify Inc. (SPOT): The original SPOT position goes back to a Series G funding round in 2015 when the company was valued at ~$8B. SPOT had an IPO in 2018. Shares started trading at ~$150 and currently go for ~$513. The position has wavered. Recent activity follows. There was a ~25% reduction during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$266 and ~$327. That was followed by a ~16% selling during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$363 and ~$502. The next quarter also saw a ~18% reduction at prices between ~$458 and ~$648. There was a ~11% further trimming during Q2 2025 at prices between ~$475 and ~$785. The last quarter also saw a similar reduction at prices between ~$616 and ~$770. There was a minor ~4% trimming this quarter. They are harvesting gains.

AppLovin (APP): The 3.45% APP stake was primarily built during Q4 2024 at prices between ~$131 and ~$402. The next quarter saw a ~30% stake increase at prices between ~$238 and ~$510. That was followed by a ~20% increase during Q2 2025 at prices between ~$201 and ~$429. The stock is now at ~$524. There was minor trimming in the last two quarters.

PDD Holdings Inc. (PDD): PDD is a 2.92% of the portfolio stake built over the three years through 2021 at prices between ~$19 and ~$197. There was a ~11% stake increase during Q2 2023 and that was followed by a ~15% increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$68 and ~$103. Q4 2023 saw a ~6% increase while in the next quarter there was a similar reduction. There was a ~14% reduction during Q2 2025 at prices between ~$88 and ~$122. The last quarter also saw a ~7% trimming at prices between ~$102 and ~$135. The stock currently trades at ~$106. This quarter saw a minor ~2% trimming.

Coupang Inc. (CPNG): CPNG had an IPO in Q1 2021. Shares started trading at ~$48 and currently go for ~$20. The 2.86% of the portfolio stake was built over the five quarters through Q1 2022 at prices between ~$17.50 and ~$48.50. The position was increased by 22% during Q4 2023 at prices between $15.17 and $18.11. That was followed by a ~16% increase in the next quarter at prices between $13.84 and $19.62. The last few quarters saw minor trimming.

Cloudflare Inc. (NET): The 2.76% NET stake was purchased during Q2 and Q3 2020 at prices between ~$21 and ~$42. The three quarters through Q2 2022 saw a ~19% selling while the next two quarters saw a ~10% increase. There was a ~23% stake increase during Q2 2023 at prices between ~$41 and ~$71. That was followed by a ~9% increase during Q2 2024 at prices between $67.43 and $97. There was a ~14% reduction during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$111 and ~$177. The last quarter saw similar selling at prices between ~$179 and ~$230. There was a ~23% further reduction this quarter at prices between ~$181 and ~$260. The stock currently trades at ~$173.

Note: they have a ~5% ownership stake in the business.

Meta Platforms (META): The 2.41% stake in META was increased by 12% during Q3 2023 at prices between ~$283 and ~$325. That was followed by a ~25% increase during the next quarter at prices between ~$288 and ~$358. Q1 2024 also saw a ~14% increase at prices between ~$344 and ~$512. There was a ~17% reduction this quarter at prices between ~$581 and ~$759. The stock currently trades at ~$659.

Microsoft Corp (MSFT): MSFT is now a 2.23% of the portfolio stake. The position saw a large ~45% stake increase during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$394 and ~$467. There was a ~14% selling during Q2 2025 at prices between ~$345 and ~$501. The stock currently trades at ~$466. The last two quarters have seen minor trimming.

Ferrari NV (RACE): RACE was a ~18M shares stake that was established in 2016 at prices between $34 and $58. The period through 2020 saw the stake reduced to ~13.5M shares at higher prices. There was a ~15% selling during Q2 2021 at prices between ~$199 and ~$217. The three quarters through Q1 2023 saw another ~30% reduction at prices between ~$180 and ~$270. The stock currently trades at ~$339. There was further selling in the last eleven quarters. They are harvesting gains.

Alnylam Pharma (ALNY): The bulk of the 0.86% of the portfolio stake in ALNY was built in 2019 at prices between ~$69 and ~$124. There was a ~20% selling during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$234 and ~$287. That was followed by a ~17% reduction during the next quarter at prices between ~$232 and ~$301. Q1 2025 also saw a ~10% selling at prices between ~$233 and ~$291. There was a ~17% reduction during the next quarter at prices between ~$206 and ~$327. The stock currently trades at ~$358. The last two quarters also saw further trimming. They are harvesting gains.

DexCom Inc. (DXCM): A small stake in DXCM was first purchased in 2012. The stake remained small till 2016 when a substantial ~6.1M share position was built in the low-20s price range. The period through 2019 saw the stake reduced to ~4.8M shares through periodic selling. Recent activity follows. There was a ~18% stake increase during Q2 2025 at prices between ~$58 and ~$88. The stock currently trades at $72.86. There was minor trimming during the last two quarters.
