data.title: Ascendis: Why I'm Sceptical On Takeover Rumours, Long-Term Targets
data.description:
Investment Overview

The stock of Ascendis Pharma (ASND) has risen in value by >1,000% over the past decade, climbing from a price of ~$20, to >$235 per share at the time of writing. Stock is also up >85% on a 1-year basis.

I first covered Ascendis in a note for Seeking Alpha in April 2023, awarding stock a Buy rating with stock trading at ~$72 per share, having collapsed in value after a delay to the FDA's regulatory review for commercial approval of TransCon PTH (palopegteriparatide) in hypoparathyroidism.

As a reminder, re its Q3 2025 quarterly report / 10Q filing:

Our TransCon technologies are designed to combine the benefits of conventional prodrug and sustained release technologies to solve the fundamental limitations seen in other approaches to extending duration of a drug’s action in the body, with the goal of developing highly differentiated product candidates based on efficacy, safety, tolerability, and convenience.

In addition to retaining the original mode of action of the parent drug and potentially supporting dosing frequency from daily up to six months or more, we believe that predictable release over time can improve treatment safety and efficacy, increase the likelihood of clinical development success, and provide intellectual property benefits.

By the time of my next note, Ascendis stock traded at $193 per share, justifying my Buy call. Ultimately, after a delay, TransCon PTH was approved in August 2024, and is also approved in Europe. It is the second product Ascendis has commercialised, and is marketed and sold as Yorvipath. Skytrofa (TransCon hGH) has also been approved to treat growth hormone deficiency, since 2021.

Announcing its Q3 earnings and business updates last November 12th, Ascendis revealed revenues of €143.1 million for YORVIPATHand €50.7 million for SKYTROFA. On a nine-month basis, the company reported $443.5m of total commercial product revenue, total revenues of $472.6m, a net loss of €61.0 million, or €1 per share, and a cash position of €539 million.

Additionally, management updated on its pipeline as follows:

TransCon® CNP (navepegritide) under FDA Priority Review for the treatment of children with achondroplasia with PDUFA date of November 30, 2025

This date has in fact been pushed back to the end of February this year, due to an FDA request for a revised protocol for the post-marketing study.

At the JP Morgan (JPM) Healthcare Conference earlier this month, Ascendis revealed in its presentation that product revenues earned in 2025 were ~€683 million, with Yorvipath earning ~€477 million, and Skytrofa ~€206 million, and that total revenue amounted to ~€720 million. Gross margin was reportedly 87%, and full-year operating expenses were estimated to be ~€762 million. Cash position was reported as ~€616 million.

During his speech at the conference, Ascendis' CEO Jan Mikkelsen told the audience:

In December last year we really had a celebration. Ascendis Pharma turned out to be 18 years. At least in Denmark, it means one thing, you throw your children away and they are financially independent. That is the key thing. You don't fund them more. That was exactly what happened at that time for Ascendis.

After 18 years. We came to the states, building on our TransCon technology, build it on our R&D engine, building on our late stage clinical development, building on what we call the last states of the rocket, how really to do global commercialization. We came to the stage where we had 2 quarters of profitable business.

In summary, Ascendis has been able to build a company with two approved products, and a third potentially to be approved this year, is generating ~$850m dollars of revenue per annum, and looks set to become net profitable, perhaps this year.

The business has risen to a market cap valuation of $14.4bn at the time of writing, and rumours surfaced towards the end of this week that it may have become a takeover target for a Swiss based Pharma company. We may speculate that e.g. Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) and Novartis (NVS), the Swiss Pharma giants, may be one of the interested parties, although nothing has been confirmed.

In the remainder of this note I'll take a closer look at Ascendis' business and speculate on whether a Pharma may be tempted to buy out Ascendis, and what price it may be prepared to pay if so, and what that may mean for the share price trajectory of the company.

Ascendis - Strong Technology, Strong Platform

Ascendis has certainly rewarded long-term shareholders' faith in its technology and products, and it is now focused on its "Vision 2030" goals, as shown in the slide below:

As we can see, the long-term revenue goal is ~€5bn of revenues, and as we can see below, multiple studies of its three drugs are ongoing, which, if successful, ought to lead to more approvals and label expansions, for example treating children, adolescents and infants, and across different territories also.

By 2035, Ascendis plans to have eight or more approved products, for example an IL-2 inhibitor and FGF-23 degrader (according to its presentation), and has established a partnership with Novo Nordisk (NVO), marketer and seller of the multi-billion dollar per annum selling weight loss / Type 2 Diabetes drugs Wegovy / Ozempic, to develop obesity / metabolic candidates, and a subsidiary company, Eyconis, to develop opthalmology candidates.

Although the company's success in bringing two products to market, and likely, a third in 2026, and achieving close to $1bn of revenues is impressive, when we consider that Ascendis' price to sales ratio is ~16.5x, and the company is not yet profitable, a >$14bn valuation of the company does feel high.

Moving from ~$850m of revenues to >$5.9bn in only a few years is a tough task - in fact, I cannot think of a pharma company (outside of the "Big Pharma" giants) that has achieved such growth in the past decade other than Moderna (MRNA), thanks to its development of a COVID vaccine.

Therefore I will preach some caution here, although if the revenue figure is met, Ascendis' valuation ought to surge past $20bn and possibly even $30bn as a result, and therefore any company paying ~$15bn for the company today would be getting a bargain.

Speaking at the JPM conference re the market opportunity in parathyroidism, the CEO stated:

In U.S., 70,000 to 90,000 (patients), which are what we call the lowest number. Some people actually believe this is 100% higher. Europe Direct 150,000 to 200,000 and international market, a huge market. And this is where we're building up a global commercialization.

So currently, we see the big driver of the revenue increase is mainly been from the U.S. side. But what we see by long-term basic that ex U.S. will take over more and more because it's much more fragmented, it takes a longer time to penetrate, but you will really see the high value because of the large patient population.

In Q1 2025, Yorvipath revenues were just €45m, so we can see that revenues are rising rapidly and we might reason that further approvals and label expansions are likely, based on a well established strong efficacy and safety record, both clinically and commercially.

Therefore as discussed, there is a lot that is derisked with Ascendis. With that said, if for any reason TransCon CNP is rejected for approval at the end of next month, I would expect a sharp market sell-off, and doubts cast over the underlying technology, which to date has been viewed very positively.

According to Ascenis, however, the pivotal study of TransCon CNP:

demonstrated significant improvements in linear growth and body proportionality, as well as benefits beyond linear growth compared to placebo

The therapy was generally well tolerated, with generally mild TEAEs and "exceeds historical benchmarks for growth without compromising safety or tolerability at 52 weeks", Ascendis says.

As we can see below, there are multiple intriguing data and approval catalysts arriving in 2026, although my feeling is that the upcoming PDUFA date in February may be the most important in terms of Ascendis' share price trajectory in 2026.

Concluding Thoughts - Am I Upgrading My Rating On Ascendis In 2026?

In my view, Ascendis is a good example of a company that is either worth significantly more, or significantly less than its current market cap valuation of ~$14.4bn.

For example, an unprofitable company trading at a P/S ratio of >15x feels overvalued, in my view, at least based on what it is achieving in the present, and in terms of what may be achieved in the future, we have to apply a discount to account for the risks that management does not meet its ambitious forecasts, that approvals are not secured, that another biotech does not launch a competing product, etc.

On the other hand, if management succeeds in driving a >40% annual revenue growth rate between 2026 - 2030, it will achieve its targets, and market dynamics, particularly with regard to hypoparathyroidism, certainly suggest the market is underpenetrated. Analysts have speculated TransCon CNP could be a "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) also. As discussed, for a $5bn revenue per annum company, a valuation of $14.5bn feels too low.

Nevertheless, I am sticking to my Hold recommendation as I don't feel there is quite enough concrete evidence to justify the ambitious revenue targets, and my estimate of what revenue the company may generate by 2030 falls short of management's estimates, simply because so few drugs drive long-term CAGR revenue of >40%.

Rumours of a takeover - also in my experience - fail to result in a confirmed takeover more often than not, and therefore, I think the best strategy is to wait at least until the February PDUFA date for TransCon CNP (I believe the market has priced in a successful outcome), and if rumours still persist at that time, re-evaluate the situation.

At this time, however, I can make more of a case for shares retreating in value - readers should also pay close attention to any revenue / income guidance also, which I suspect may slightly underwhelm the market given the stated long-term targets - than I can for further gains being realised, at this time, so my best call is Hold - by the end of 2026, the overall picture around whether the Vision 2030 goals are achievable ought to be substantially clearer.

data.title: HFRO: Resilient Real Estate Fund But Struggling In High Interest Rate Environment
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered Highland Opportunities and Income Fund (HFRO), I issued a hold rating due to the challenging macroenvironment. Since my last coverage, the Fed has initiated interest rate cuts, and this has created the expectation for lower interest rates over the next twelve months. Therefore, I wanted to revisit the fund's overall value proposition and ability to navigate challenging conditions going forward. The fund primarily focuses on real estate securities, which has been a sector that continues to lag behind the rest of the market.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that HFRO's share price has increased by nearly 9.6%. The fund has been able to hold onto some of the positive price growth experienced around mid-2025. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 19.6% over the same time frame. HFRO now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 7.5% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. Based on the latest financials, the fund's dividend payouts are likely to remain stable despite variable coverage.

The fund continues to trade at a discounted price to NAV valuation, which can be an indication that it's a solid time to accumulate shares. However, a position in HFRO makes the most sense for investors that believe interest rates will continue to decline over the next twelve months. In a scenario where interest rates do not decline, the fund's risk profile can increase. For instance, HFRO's dividends may get cut if the underlying earnings do not improve over the next year. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that HFRO implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, HFRO has total managed assets of $880M that are allocated across a blended portfolio of assets. The fund's primary objective is to grow capital while providing a high current income. In order to achieve this goal, the fund chooses to focus on real estate securities and different forms of debt securities. HFRO allocates at least 25% of its assets to securities that are directly secured by physical real estate. The portfolio's breakdown by investment type is as follows:

  • Real Estate: 65.7%
  • Equity: 22.1%
  • Loan: 11.6%
  • Collateralized Loan Obligations: 0.7%

What stands out to me is that HFRO also has some direct equity exposure. This equity exposure aligns the fund to see capital appreciation during favorable market cycles for real estate. Expanding on this, we can see that HFRO's real estate is also spread across different operating segments, such as multifamily, life sciences, and storage properties. Although the real estate investments span across different operating areas, real estate as a whole generally shares the same vulnerability to higher interest rates. The sector is arguably one of the most dependent on access to cheap debt financing, and the current environment of elevated rates could be suppressing performance.

When looking at the top positions within the fund, we can see that HFRO provides a clear breakdown of its positions and how much of the holding is positioned as equity or debt. The portfolio is primarily allocated to a pool of private entities, which may be attractive for investors looking to get access to securities that aren't commonly available. These private entities also have the potential for growth, but they lack the same transparency that public holdings would provide.

Valuation & Performance Insights

At the time of my last coverage, HFRO traded at a large discount to NAV of 42.21%. Despite the share price increase over the last few months, the fund now trades at a slightly larger discount to NAV of 47%. This larger discount can be an indication of an underlying price disconnect and can be an opportunity for long-term investors to accumulate on the weakness. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that HFRO currently trades well below the average price-to-NAV level of peers within the same industry.

In order to get a better understanding of why the large discount to NAV pricing exists, I thought it would be helpful to review the latest earnings. While these earnings may be a bit dated, they reveal some clear vulnerabilities of the fund that must be addressed. For instance, investments at fair value continue to decline, while the total debt outstanding simultaneously increases. HFRO's net assets now sit around the lowest levels seen over the last few years, while the debt-to-equity ratio has more than doubled to 0.39x since Q3 of 2023.

With the additional debt taken on, HFRO has been able to increase its total investment income up to $11.8M. However, the actual net investment income amounts to $8.9M, which is a less impressive increase from the prior quarter's net investment income of $7.5M. This implies that the fund's operating costs are elevated and may be shrinking the internal spread.

As part of the fund's strategy, HFRO utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings. The use of leverage currently amounts to 27.81% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$245M. During this period of elevated interest rates, I would consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage since the fund's operating spread between the cost of its debt and the yield collected from securities may be thinner. Additionally, leverage can always amplify losses if allocated to securities that don't perform as anticipated. This use of leverage has ultimately amplified the fund's sensitivity to interest rates, as we can see below. The idea is that future interest rate cuts will lead to optimism around real estate, as well as improve operating spreads, which can help HFRO's share price regain some positive momentum.

Lastly, the fund's priority on distributions does not actually lead to higher returns over time. The combination of the use of leverage, sensitivity to interest rates, and inconsistent earnings means that the fund can find itself in a position where it is paying out more than it actually earns. The end result is a share price that can decline more rapidly than a more traditional sector-tracking ETF, such as the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLRE). Additionally, the focus on income-producing securities means that HFRO's ability to capture price growth is fairly limited compared to a fund like XLRE.

As we can see below, HFRO's total return since inception sits at a loss of 21.3%, even when including all distributions paid. The share price erosion has been severe since inception, with the share price being down more than 60% over the same time frame. The fund severely underperforms XLRE over the same time frame, so the focus on income may translate to underperformance if the health of the real estate sector doesn't improve.

Dividend is Too Generous

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0385 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.5%. Looking at the dividend history below, we can see that distributions were severely reduced at the start of 2024, which is likely due to the poor earnings of the fund. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by three months, we get a quarterly payout of $0.1155 per share. Referring back to the same earnings report, HFRO was able to generate net investment income per share of $0.16. This indicates that earnings currently support the distribution at a coverage rate of 139%. Therefore, I don't think the dividend is under the threat of a reduction in the near term.

Despite the large distribution cut in the past, I believe that payouts may still be a bit too generous. This is because the fund is essentially paying out most of its earnings, and this limits the ability to use free capital towards new investments. Additionally, the fund's underlying NAV continues its downward trend over the last year. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • Q3 2024: $12.96 per share
  • Q4 2024: $12.00 per share
  • Q1 2025: $12.35 per share
  • Q2 2025: $11.38 per share
  • Q3 2025: $11.48 per share

Lastly, investors should consider the fact that distributions may not offer tax efficiency. Since the distributions are being supported by real estate securities and interest income, the distributions are likely classified as ordinary dividends, which have the highest tax burden for investors. Unfortunately, there is not an updated section 19(a) notice that is available at this time to confirm this.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that HFRO remains a hold at this time. The fund trades at a massive discount to NAV valuation due to the challenging interest rate environment. The latest earnings report indicates that net investment income still supports the distributions, but there is certainly room for improvement. For instance, the fund's total outstanding debt has increased substantially over the last few years, and total net assets have declined. Since interest rates remain elevated, the fund's operating spread may be thin due to the inclusion of leverage and floating rate exposure. However, future interest rate cuts can potentially serve as a catalyst for growth in the future. A lower cost of debt may improve the fund's operating spread and increase optimism of the real estate sector.

Portfolio Positioning

Portfolio anchored on quality amid elevated market valuations

We continue to maintain a balanced, quality-anchored positioning in the portfolio. Our bottom-up fundamental assessment drives this risk-aware exposure across industries that we believe will benefit from long-term thematic tailwinds. We also maintain ample exposure to defensive holdings that will preserve capital should markets experience increased volatility or a pullback in the coming quarters.

The Fund's largest sector overweights are in Financials, Health Care and Information Technology. Our three largest underweights remain in Energy (no exposure), Consumer Staples and Consumer Discretionary.

Within Financials, our overall portfolio weight and stock level exposures remain largely unchanged, with majority exposure in companies that do not take balance sheet risk and are likely to be less affected by an economic slowdown.

During the quarter, we modestly increased our overweight in the Health Care sector primarily driven by the attractive risk/reward opportunity, especially as the sector emerges from several headwinds faced over the past three years.

Our overweight in Information Technology was modestly lower versus the third quarter as we continue to realize gains in select long-term secular winners. While we remain constructive on the sector’s structural growth drivers, we are also cognizant of valuation levels, particularly as market gains have broadened across a wider array of beneficiaries in recent quarters.

Across both Consumer Discretionary and Consumer Staples, our underweight positioning remained largely unchanged over the quarter. We believe our overweight in Financials, particularly in Payments, continues to provide an attractive risk-adjusted means of participating in consumer spending trends.

Additionally, we reduced our underweight positioning in Utilities and Industrials with the addition of CMS Energy and Waste Management. CMS’s investment plans are expected to accelerate earnings growth over the coming years, within a constructive regulatory environment. Waste Management’s wide moat, attractive valuation and commitment to environmental innovation offer a defensive business model with long-term upside potential.

Outlook

Market environment favors continued outperformance for value stocks

Value stocks outperformed growth stocks in the fourth quarter as the Russell 1000 Value index rose 3.81% compared to a 1.12% rise in the Russell 1000 Growth index. We expect the current market environment to continue favoring value stocks. High market valuations for growth stocks, a relatively benign economic backdrop, potential for lower rates and stimulative policy levers are all supportive of attractive risk/reward potential in value stocks.

Moreover, we believe the once-in-a-generation AI investments being made today will, over time, drive productivity gains, business model innovation and earnings growth across a much broader range of industries than is being appreciated by investors today. AI has the potential to impact every sector, not just technology, akin to earlier waves of automation and technology adoption. The timing of these benefits admittedly remains uncertain, which underpins the Fund's diversified exposure across sectors and across time—some will benefit this year, some over three years or longer. However, what we believe is more certain is that regardless of the sector, companies that have a strong track record of investing in innovation and the scale to justify these upfront investments will eventually emerge stronger through AI. This was true in prior innovation cycles, and this time will be no different.

The high-quality companies we own in the Value Equity Fund—across Banking, Insurance, Retail, Pharma, Medical Devices or Agricultural Equipment, among others—all possess the scale to invest ahead of peers and a demonstrated ability to translate investments into enduring shareholder value.

Our time-tested investment process with a disciplined focus on assessing both risk and return has served us well over the last two decades. As we enter the Fund’s 21st year, we remain confident that the portfolio is well positioned to navigate both macroeconomic and thematic crosscurrents. We will continue to focus on identifying and investing in quality businesses trading at attractive relative valuations in the pursuit of strong long-term returns.

Allocation Effect: the allocation effect in performance attribution measures how much of a portfolio's outperformance or underperformance against a benchmark comes from the manager's decision to overweight or underweight specific sectors or asset classes, rather than from picking individual stocks within them.
